elements.sent-2411-001: We introduce a stochastic model of a population with overlapping generations and arbitrary levels of self-fertilization versus outcrossing.
elements.sent-2411-002: We study how the global graph of reproductive relationships, or pedigree, influences the genealogical relationships of a sample of two gene copies at a genetic locus.
elements.sent-2411-003: Specifically, we consider a diploid Moran model with constant population size $N$ over time, in which a proportion of offspring are produced by selfing.
elements.sent-2411-004: We show that the conditional distribution of the pairwise coalescence time at a single locus given the random pedigree converges to a limit law as $N$ tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-005: This limit law generally differs from the corresponding traditional result obtained by averaging over all possible pedigrees.
elements.sent-2411-006: We describe three different behaviors in the limit depending on the relative strengths, from large to small, of selfing versus outcrossing: partial selfing, limited outcrossing, and negligible outcrossing.
elements.sent-2411-007: In the case of partial selfing, coalescence times are related to the Kingman coalescent, similar to what is found in traditional analyses.
elements.sent-2411-008: In the the case of limited outcrossing, the retained pedigree information forms a random graph coming from a fragmentation-coagulation process, with the limiting coalescence times given by the meeting times of random walks on this graph.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the case of negligible outcrossing, which represents complete or nearly complete selfing, coalescence times are determined entirely by the fixed times to common ancestry of diploid individuals in the pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-010: Modes of reproduction and genetic transmission are fundamental aspects of evolution \citep{CharlesworthAndWright2001,GleminEtAl2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-011: In this work, we consider autogamy or self-fertilization---hereafter just `selfing'---in which meiosis occurs then a zygote is formed by the union of two gametes from the same individual.
elements.sent-2411-012: This form of inbreeding has been especially important in the evolution of plants \citep{Jain1976,UyenoyamaEtAl1993,Charlesworth2006,OlsenEtAl2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-013: The common alternative to selfing in plants and animals is outcrossing, in which zygotes are formed by the union of two gametes from different individuals.
elements.sent-2411-014: The selfing rate, defined here as the probability that an individual is produced by selfing, ranges from $0$ to $1$.
elements.sent-2411-015: We describe three distinct kinds of coalescent models which hold for different magnitudes of the selfing rate when the process of coalescence is conditioned on the population pedigree, or the organismal genealogy of the population \citep{ball1990 .}
elements.sent-2411-016: This important latent variable has been mostly ignored in theoretical population genetics; see Section~\ref{sec:pedigrees of this paper and \citet{DFBW24 .}}
elements.sent-2411-017: The conditional coalescent models we describe exist in the limit as the population size $N$ tends to infinity in the same way that the Wright-Fisher diffusion and the corresponding standard neutral coalescent model exist in this limit \citep{kingman1982,Mohle1999,Ewens2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-018: We use $\alpha_N$ to denote the selfing rate, with explicit dependence on $N$ so we can control the relative importance of selfing in the model.
elements.sent-2411-019: Our results for coalescence in large populations conditional on the pedigree depend strongly on how $\alpha_N$ changes as $N\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-020: Most previous analyses, in addition to not conditioning on the pedigree, have assumed a fixed selfing rate.
elements.sent-2411-021: This rate is often denoted $s$, though various notations have been adopted; e.g.\ $l$ in \citet{Haldane1924 , $h$ in \citet{Wright1951 , $\beta$ in \citet{Pollak1987 and $\alpha$ in \citet{Karlin1968a,Karlin1968b .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-022: As \citet{Uyenoyama2024 recently emphasized, some authors use $s$ for the entire selfing rate while others use it to measure selfing in excess of a background rate $1/N$ due to random mating.}
elements.sent-2411-023: Our model includes these as special cases, namely if we assume $\alpha_N = s$ or $\alpha_N = s + (1-s)/N$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-024: These two models with fixed $s$ make the same predictions in the limit and both have been called `partial self-fertilization' or `partial selfing'.
elements.sent-2411-025: We use $\alpha\in[0,1]$ to denote the limit of $\alpha_N$ as $N\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-026: Conditioning coalescence on the pedigree reveals three markedly different behaviors near the boundary $\alpha=1$, depending on how quickly $\alpha_N\to 1$.
elements.sent-2411-027: The critical case, which we call `limited outcrossing', is when $1-\alpha_N$ is of order $1/N$.
elements.sent-2411-028: Then the conditional coalescent process is characterized by a system of coalescing random walks on a random ancestral graph like the ones previously described for recombination \citep{Griffiths1991,GriffithsAndMarjoram1997 and selection \citep{kroneneuhauser1997,NeuhauserAndKrone1997 .}}
elements.sent-2411-029: If outcrossing is even less frequent, for example $1-\alpha_N$ of order $1/N^2$, the limiting conditional coalescent process is fully determined by the random pedigree of the population, which becomes just a Kingman coalescent tree connecting diploid individuals.
elements.sent-2411-031: If outcrossing is more frequent than in the critical case, for example $1-\alpha_N$ of order $1/\sqrt{N}$, the limiting conditional coalescent model resembles the previously described (unconditional) coalescent model of \citet{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 and \citet{Mohle1998a .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: Here we adopt the previous term `partial selfing'.
elements.sent-2411-033: The latter model holds for all $\alpha\in[0,1]$ as long as the rate of approach to $\alpha=1$ is not too fast, whereas limited outcrossing and negligible outcrossing hold just for $\alpha=1$.
elements.sent-2411-034: In the next two sections, we review previous works which generally use $s$ for the fixed selfing rate, take $s\in[0,1]$ and do not condition on the population pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-035: We will reserve our notation $\alpha_N$ for Section~\ref{sec:pedigrees and later.}
elements.sent-2411-036: In Section~\ref{sec:pedigrees , we explain how previous population-genetic models in fact average over pedigrees.}
elements.sent-2411-037: In Section~\ref{sec:broad and Section~\ref{sec:specific we use $s$ to maintain continuity with what one finds in much of the evolutionary and population-genetic literature.}}
elements.subsection-2411-001: Prevalence, causes and consequences of selfing in evolution
elements.sent-2411-038: Although the ability to self-fertilize is rare among animals \citep{JarneAndAuld2006,AviseAndMank2009,EscobarEtAl2011 it is found in other taxa including eukaryotic microbes and marine invertebrates \citep{SassonAndRyan2017,YadavEtAl2023 and is common in plants \citep{AbbottAndGomes1989,HartfieldEtAl2017,TeterinaEtAl2023 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: About $10$-$15$\% of plant species are predominantly selfing \citep{WrightEtAl2013 as are two of the best-studied genetic model species, Arabidopsis thaliana and Caenorhabditis elegans \citep{AbbottAndGomes1989,SellingerEtAl2020,BarriereAndFelix2005 .}}
elements.sent-2411-040: The empirical distribution of selfing rates among plant species is markedly bimodal, with relatively fewer species having $s\in(0.2,0.8)$ \citep{SchemskeAndLande1985,VoglerAndKalisz2001,GoodwillieEtAl2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-041: Genetical models of inbreeding depression can explain this bimodality \citep{LandeAndSchemske1985,CharlesworthAndWillis2009 .}
elements.sent-2411-042: But, whether selfing is favored over outcrossing or vice versa in a given species depends on a number of ecological and evolutionary factors \citep{Charlesworth2006,WrightEtAl2013 .}
elements.sent-2411-043: Selfing is a form of inbreeding which has three important consequences for the distribution of genetic variation.
elements.sent-2411-045: His result may best be described using single-locus probabilities of identity by descent.
elements.sent-2411-046: See \citet{Rousset2002 for a synthetic treatment of identity by descent and other measures of inbreeding and relatedness.}
elements.sent-2411-047: In terms of Wright's individual inbreeding coefficient $F$ \citep{Wright1931,Wright1951 , what \citet{Haldane1924 showed was that the reduction in the proportion of heterozygous individuals in the population at equilibrium is given by $F=s/(2-s)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-048: A simple coalescent interpretation is that $F$ is the probability the two alleles or gene copies at a locus in an individual coalesce in the recent ancestry of the individual, rather than being separated into different individuals by an outcrossing event \citep{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-049: Second, selfing reduces the effective rate of recombination.
elements.sent-2411-050: Recombination is similarly reduced in finite populations \citep{GoldingAndStrobeck1980,VitalisAndCouvet2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-051: Backward in time, a recombination event places ancestral genetic material onto two chromosomes.
elements.sent-2411-052: These are necessarily in the same parental individual which might itself have been produced by selfing.
elements.sent-2411-053: Then the same coefficient $F$ is the probability the recombination event gets healed or repaired by a coalescent event within the individual's recent ancestry.
elements.sent-2411-054: As a result, only a fraction $1-F=2(1-s)/(2-s)$ of recombination events are observable.
elements.sent-2411-055: Third, selfing generates non-random associations of diploid genotypes at different loci, even if the loci are unlinked.
elements.sent-2411-056: As \citet{Smith2023 recently emphasized, both multi-locus homozygotes and multi-locus heterozygotes are overrepresented.}
elements.sent-2411-057: A coalescent interpretation of the identity disequilibrium for two unlinked loci is as follows.
elements.sent-2411-058: Let $Y_i$ be an indicator variable, equal to $1$ if the gene copies at locus $i$ in a particular individual coalesce in its recent ancestry, and equal to $0$ otherwise.
elements.sent-2411-059: The recent ancestry of a randomly sampled individual is given by the number of generations of selfing in its ancestral line before the first outcrossing event.
elements.sent-2411-060: As \citet{WeirAndCockerham1973 note, this formulation originates with \citet{CockerhamAndRawlings1967 .}}
elements.sent-2411-061: For an individual with $k$ generations of selfing in its immediate ancestry, the probability the two gene copies at a locus remain distinct (i.e.\ do not coalesce) in any of these generations is $2^{-k}$.
elements.sent-2411-062: If $U$ denotes the random number of selfing generations for an individual, \begin{equation}\PP(U=k) = s^k (1-s) \qquad k = 0,1,2,\ldots \label{eq:PUk}\end{equation} and the coefficient $F$ can be seen as the average \begin{equation}F \coloneq \mathbb{E}\left[Y_i\right] = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(1-2^{-k}\right) s^k (1-s) = \frac{s}{2-s} . \label{eq:Fdef}\end{equation} The covariance of $Y_i$ and $Y_j$ for two unlinked loci, which are conditionally independent given $k$, is \begin{equation}\mathrm{Cov}{\left[Y_i,Y_j\right]} = \sum_{k=0}^{\infty} \left(1-2^{-k}\right)^2 s^k (1-s) - \left(\frac{s}{2-s}\right)^2 \label{eq:covxixj}\end{equation} which is identical to \eqref{eq:sigma2F , since $\sigma^2_F \coloneq \mathrm{Var {\left[Y_i\right]}$ is also given by \eqref{eq:covxixj .}}}
elements.sent-2411-063: Interpreting identity disequilibria as covariances is sensible because they occur between loci.
elements.sent-2411-064: In anticipation of later results conditional on the population pedigree, it is important that \eqref{eq:PUk here is intended as the theoretical distribution for a single individual sampled at random from a given population.}
elements.sent-2411-065: In keeping with \citet{Haldane1949 , \eqref{eq:covxixj is an average which quantifies variation among individuals.}}
elements.sent-2411-066: Owing to these three effects---increased homozygosity, reduced recombination, and identity disequilibrium---partially selfing populations follow different evolutionary paths than randomly mating populations.
elements.sent-2411-067: Selfing alters the evolution of the recombination rate \citep{CharlesworthEtAl1977,RozeAndLenormand2005,StetsenkoAndRoze2022 , leads to apparent or spurious multi-locus signals of selection \citep{CockerhamAndRawlings1967,OhtaAndCockerham1974,Charlesworth1991 , determines levels of inbreeding depression \citep{Nei1965,HanscheEtAl1966,Campbell1986,CharlesworthEtAl1990,LandeEtAl1994,SchultzAndWillis1995,AbuAwadAndRoze2018 , and in many ways alters the responses of populations to selection \citep{Kondrashov1985,WrightAndCockerham1985,CharlesworthEtAl1993,NordborgEtAl1996,Willis1996,Kelly1999a,Kelly1999b,Kelly2007,LandeAndPorcher2015,Roze2016,Sachdeva2019 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-068: Among these evolutionary studies, \citet{CockerhamAndRawlings1967 , \citet{Campbell1986 , \citet{Kelly1999a,Kelly1999b,Kelly2007 and \citet{LandeAndPorcher2015 explicitly model the variation in the number of selfing generations in the recent ancestry of individuals, given here under neutrality by \eqref{eq:PUk .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-069: The effects of selfing on the distribution of genetic variation are also important determinants of the evolution of the selfing rate \citep{CharlesworthEtAl1990,UyenoyamaAndWaller1991a,UyenoyamaAndWaller1991b,UyenoyamaAndWaller1991c,AbuAwadAndRoze2020 but in themselves do not explain the prevalence of selfing.}
elements.sent-2411-070: In the absence of other evolutionary forces, selfing is strongly favored over outcrossing \citep{Fisher1941,Moran1962,KarlinAndMcGregor1974,Nagylaki1976,Lloyd1979,Wells1979 .}
elements.sent-2411-071: As \citet{MaynardSmith1971 observed in discussing the evolution of sexual reproduction or meiosis, outcrossing individuals are at a disadvantage because they transmit only half of their genome to each offspring.}
elements.sent-2411-072: The comparison in \citet{MaynardSmith1971 was to parthenogenesis or asexual reproduction, but selfing is a way to alleviate this ``cost of sex'' in organisms which necessarily undergo meiosis.}
elements.sent-2411-073: Thus, while \citet{WilliamsAndMitton1973 attributed this cost to meiosis itself, \citet{Uyenoyama1986 more precisely reckoned it a cost of outcrossing.}}
elements.sent-2411-074: The fact that outcrossing is so common can be explained in genetical terms by the avoidance of inbreeding depression \citep{CharlesworthAndWillis2009,BrownAndKelly2020 or the purging or deleterious mutations in the presence of recombination \citep{Kondrashov1985,KeightleyAndOtto2006,Kamran‐DisfaniAndAgrawal2014 .}}
elements.sent-2411-075: In addition, many ecological factors affect the evolutionary dynamics of selfing species \citep{Jain1976,Solbrig1976 .}
elements.sent-2411-076: Pioneering work by \citet{Baker1955 and \citet{Stebbins1957 showed that selfing may be favored when the usual modes of cross-fertilization are blocked, in plants for example by excessive rainfall or lack of pollinators, or when the environment presents opportunities for colonization starting from one or a few individuals, for example if rare long-distance dispersal events can access open habitat or if there is rapid turnover of suitable habitat patches.}}
elements.sent-2411-077: Selfing species with their characteristic suite of phenotypes have evolved many times from outcrossing ancestors, prompting questions about whether it might be an evolutionary ``dead end'' \citep{Stebbins1957,Ornduff1969,SicardAndLenhard2011,WrightEtAl2013,Cutter2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-078: Within a single species, subpopulations of selfers are commonly restricted to patches at the margins of the habitat \citep{Baker1955,Stebbins1957 , as for example in the case of Leavenworthia alabamica detailed in \citet{Busch2005 .}}
elements.subsection-2411-002: Population genetics of selfing under neutrality
elements.sent-2411-079: Our goal in this work is to understand how selfing affects the sampling structure of gene genealogies under neutrality when the process of coalescence is conditioned on the pedigree of the population.
elements.sent-2411-080: We ask whether results conditional on the pedigree differ from those of previous theoretical treatments which have disregarded this feature of the population.
elements.sent-2411-081: For simplicity we use a diploid Moran model of reproduction \citep{Moran1958,Moran1962,linder2009,coron2022pedigree , which we will describe in Section~\ref{section: moran_model .}}
elements.sent-2411-082: Previous theoretical treatments, including those we review here, have assumed Wright-Fisher reproduction \citep{Fisher1930,Wright1931 .}
elements.sent-2411-083: Based on the considerations of Section~\ref{sec:broad , it may be that selfing species are even less likely than outcrossing ones to meet the assumptions of standard models of population genetics.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Both the Wright-Fisher model and the Moran model apply to a single well mixed population of constant in size $N$ without selection.
elements.sent-2411-085: The simplest application relevant to selfing is to diploid, monoecious, randomly mating organisms, in which case selfing happens with probability $1/N$.
elements.sent-2411-086: When $N$ can be assumed to be large, both models converge to the Wright-Fisher diffusion with its corresponding coalescent process \citep{kingman1982,Hudson1983a,tajima1983,Mohle1999,Ewens2004 which exist in the limit $N\to\infty$ with time measured in units proportional to $N$ generations.}
elements.sent-2411-087: This provides a common framework for studying more complicated populations, as deviations from this simple null model.
elements.sent-2411-088: Selfing is one such deviation, which has been incorporated into the model by assuming that the selfing rate $s$ is a fixed constant when the population size $N$ tends to infinity.
elements.sent-2411-089: Let us call these probabilities $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-090: For large $N$, \citet{Pollak1987 found that the expression for $\Phi_2$ was identical to that for a randomly mating population, if $N_e = N/(1+F) = (2-s) N / 2$ is used in place of $N$.}
elements.sent-2411-091: As $s$ increases from $0$ to $1$, $N_e$ decreases from $N$ to $N/2$, effectively taking the value of $\Phi_2$ from that for a diploid (randomly mating) population to that for a haploid population.
elements.sent-2411-092: The separation of time scales can be seen in the relationship \citet{Pollak1987 found between $\Phi_1$ and $\Phi_2$, namely that $\Phi_1 = F + (1-F) \Phi_2$, which may best be understood in terms of subsequent descriptions of the coalescent process.}
elements.sent-2411-093: The fast process occurs when one or more pairs of lineages are in the same individual(s).
elements.sent-2411-094: They showed (though without a formal proof) that in the limit $N\to\infty$, if a sample of size $n$ contains $2m$ gene copies together as pairs in $m$ individuals and $n-2m$ gene copies each in different individuals, there is an instantaneous adjustment in which a random number $X \sim binomial(m,F)$ of the $m$ pairs coalesce.
elements.sent-2411-095: The rest of the ancestry begins with the resulting $n-X$ lineages and is given by the standard neutral coalescent process \citep{kingman1982,Hudson1983a,tajima1983 , if time is measured in units of $2N_e = (2-s) N$ generations.}
elements.sent-2411-096: It was in this context that \citet{Mohle1998a proved an important general result \citep[Lemma 1, Theorem 1]{Mohle1998a for Markov processes with two time scales.}}
elements.sent-2411-097: Owing to the duality relation between the coalescent and diffusion models \citep{Mohle1999 this result can be seen as the backward-time counterpart to the result of \citet{EthierAndNagylaki1980 cited by \citet{Pollak1987 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-098: For the selfing model with fixed $s$ as $N\to\infty$, this gave rigorous justification to the model of \citet{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 : whenever a pair of lineages is in the same individual, they coalesce with probability $F=s/(2-s)$ instantaneously, otherwise ending up in different individuals with probability $1-F$, while samples from different individuals obey the standard neutral coalescent process with time in units of $(2-s) N$ generations \citep{Mohle1998a .}}
elements.sent-2411-099: A somewhat different approach to the coalescent process with partial selfing was taken by \citet{Fu1997 , who approximated the single-generation backward-time transition probabilities for the number of ancestral lineages and the number of individuals containing them, up to order $1/N$, then used these in an algorithm to simulate coalescent ancestries.}
elements.sent-2411-100: Time remains in generations, and the algorithm generates coalescence times of some few generations for pairs of gene copies within individuals.
elements.sent-2411-101: Something like this could be done by developing an algorithm for the pre-limiting ancestral process described in sections 3.2 and 3.3 in \citet{Mohle1998a .}
elements.sent-2411-102: But it would not produce identical results, because \citet{Mohle1998a , also \citet{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 , used $s$ for the total proportion of selfing, while \citet{Fu1997 used $s$ for the excess proportion of selfing, for a total of $s+(1-s)/N$.}}}
elements.subsection-2411-003: Accounting for the pedigree of the population
elements.sent-2411-103: The pedigree of a population is a graph representing the set of reproductive relationships of all individuals in the population for all time.
elements.sent-2411-104: Previous studies of the population-genetic effects of selfing, including those reviewed in Section~\ref{sec:specific , have followed standard practice in population genetics by averaging over all possible outcomes of reproduction, which is equivalent to averaging over the pedigree.}
elements.sent-2411-105: See \citet{DFBW24 for a recent detailed discussion of the issues surrounding this tradition in population genetics, which we review briefly here.}
elements.sent-2411-106: Averaging over outcomes of reproduction is often done implicitly, for example by stating that two ancestral lines coalesce with probability $1/(2N)$ each generation under the diploid monoecious Wright-Fisher model.
elements.sent-2411-107: Sometimes it is explicit, for example in equation (4.1) in \citet{kingman1982 for the coalescence probability under Cannings' haploid exchangeable model \citep{Cannings1974 .}}
elements.sent-2411-108: For diploids in particular, the practice of averaging is highly questionable because all reproductive outcomes in the population for all time are encoded in a fixed structure, the genealogy or pedigree of the population through which all genetic transmission must also have occurred \citep{ball1990 .}
elements.sent-2411-109: In the most common application of coalescent models, which is to describe the distribution of gene genealogies or genetic variation among loci, the practice of averaging over reproductive outcomes is plainly wrong because the same pedigree holds for all loci.
elements.sent-2411-110: A better-justified starting point for coalescent theory is to condition the ancestral genetic process on the pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-111: This gives the correct sampling structure for multiple unlinked loci: conditionally independent realizations of the process of genetic transmission within the same population/pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-112: The sampling structure implicit in traditionally formulated coalescent models is that each locus has its own pedigree, as if each locus was sampled from an independent population.
elements.sent-2411-113: It turns out that the standard neutral coalescent model, at least, can still be applied because the coalescent model conditional on the pedigree is the same as the standard coalescent model for populations in the domain of the Kingman coalescent \citep{TyukinThesis2015,DFBW24 .}
elements.sent-2411-114: The reason is that pedigrees in large well mixed populations have a characteristic mixing time of $\log_2 N$ generations \citep{Chang1999,DerridaEtAl1999,BartonAndEtheridge2011,wakeleyetal2012 .}
elements.sent-2411-115: But it is not true in general that extensions of the standard neutral coalescent model are similarly applicable to coalescent processes conditional on pedigrees generated under the corresponding population models \citep{WakeleyEtAl2016,WiltonEtAl2017,DFBW24 .}
elements.sent-2411-116: The coalescent models of partial selfing in \citet{Fu1997 , \citet{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 and \citet{Mohle1998a average over the process of reproduction and thus implicitly over the pedigree.}}}
elements.sent-2411-117: One consequence of instead conditioning on the pedigree is that each sampled individual has its own realized number of generations of selfing before the first outcrossing event in its ancestry.
elements.sent-2411-118: This, as we will show, is a single draw from the distribution \eqref{eq:PUk .}
elements.sent-2411-119: The end result of averaging is clear in previous approaches: $F$ in \eqref{eq:Fdef is such an average.}
elements.sent-2411-120: The averaging procedure is illustrated by the matrix $A^m$ in \citet[p.
elements.sent-2411-121: For example, \begin{equation}\left(A^m\right)_{32} = (1-s)\frac{1-(s/2)^m}{1-s/2}\end{equation} is the probability that the ancestors of two gene copies currently in the same individual remain distinct and are in two different individuals in past generation $m$.
elements.sent-2411-122: This can be expressed as a sum over the distribution \eqref{eq:PUk , namely \begin{equation}\left(A^m\right)_{32} = \sum_{k=0}^{m-1} \left(\frac{1}{2}\right)^k s^k (1-s) . \label{eq:mohleaverging}\end{equation} Lemma 1 and Theorem 1 of \citet{Mohle1998a use the limit of the fast process, $P \coloneq \lim_{m\to\infty A^m$, in which \eqref{eq:mohleaverging converges to $2(1-s)/(2-s)=1-F$, or $\mathbb{E}\left[1-Y_i\right]$ for the indicator random variable $Y_i$ defined in Section~\ref{sec:broad .}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-123: Partial selfing is just one of three possibilities when the coalescent process is conditioned on the pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-124: Here we reintroduce our notation, in which $\alpha_N$ denotes the selfing rate in a population of size $N$, and $\alpha$ denotes its value in the limit $N\to\infty$ or in the special case that $\alpha_N=\alpha$ is constant.
elements.sent-2411-125: When selfing is the primary mode of reproduction, an entirely new sort of model emerges in the limit, one which is completely invisible in the unconditional pedigree-averaging process.
elements.sent-2411-126: This novel limit process occurs when the outcrossing rate $1-\alpha_N$ is of order $N^{-1}$.
elements.sent-2411-127: It is parameterized by $\lambda\coloneq\lim_{N\to\infty N(1-\alpha_N)$, which we note is the same way mutation rates are assumed to scale in the Wright-Fisher diffusion and corresponding standard neutral coalescent model \citep{kingman1982,Hudson1983a,tajima1983,Ewens2004 and how migration rates are scaled in the structured coalescent model \citep{Takahata1988,Notohara1990,Herbots1997 .}}}

elements.sent-2411-129: Figure~\ref{fig:1 shows simulation results of pedigrees and pairwise coalescence times for the model of selfing used by \citet{Mohle1998a , which corresponds to $\alpha_N=\alpha$ constant in our model.}}

elements.section-2411-002: A discrete-time diploid Moran model with selfing
elements.sent-2411-156: We consider a diploid, monoecious, panmictic (well mixed and randomly mating) population of constant $N$ over time.
elements.sent-2411-158: The population has overlapping generations and changes at discrete time-steps.
elements.sent-2411-159: At each time-step, a randomly chosen individual dies and is replaced by an offspring produced in one of two ways.
elements.sent-2411-160: With probability $1-\alpha_N$ the offspring has two distinct parents; with probability $\alpha_N$ the offspring has exactly one parent.
elements.sent-2411-161: The parent(s) are chosen uniformly at random from the previous time-step.
elements.sent-2411-162: In the case of two parents, these are chosen without replacement.
elements.sent-2411-163: The reproduction events at different time-steps are independent and identically distributed.
elements.sent-2411-164: Explicitly, for each non-negative integer $k\in\mathbb{Z _+=\{0, 1, 2, 3, ...\}$, the reproduction event between consecutive time-steps $k$ and $k+1$ in the past is as follows:.}
elements.sent-2411-165: A triplet containing the two parents and one offspring $(\pi_{k,1 ,\, \pi_{k,2 ,\, \kappa_k) \in I ^ 3$ is chosen uniformly at random with $\pi_{k,1}\neq \pi_{k,2}$ (i.e.\ the parents are distinct).}}
elements.sent-2411-166: The offspring $\kappa_k$ is an individual in time-step $k$, while the offspring's parents are the individuals in time-step $k + 1$ with labels $\pi_{k,1}$ and $\pi_{k,2}$.
elements.sent-2411-167: The offspring has two genes copies, one inherited from each parent.
elements.sent-2411-168: Genetically, the offspring is produced according to Mendel's laws, which means each of the two gene copies in a parent is equally likely to be the one transmitted to the offspring.
elements.sent-2411-169: An example outcrossing event in a population of size $N=7$ is depicted below.
elements.sent-2411-170: The offspring $\kappa_k$ in time-step $k$ is the offspring of a single individual (the parent) with label $\pi_{k}$ at time-step $k + 1$.
elements.sent-2411-171: This can be viewed as having $\pi_{k,2 =\pi_{k,1}$ in the previous reproduction event.}
elements.sent-2411-172: As above, the offspring is produced according to Mendel's laws which means each of the two gene copies in the parent is independently and equally likely to be inherited by each of those of the offspring.
elements.sent-2411-173: An example selfing event in a population of size $N=7$ is depicted below.
elements.sent-2411-174: The reproduction events at different time-steps are independent.
elements.sent-2411-175: Note that at time-step $k$, the offspring $\kappa_k$ is a new individual introduced and it replaces the one with the same label in the previous step $k+1$ (death-birth for the label $\kappa_k$); all the remaining $N-1$ individuals in time-step $k$ are the same individuals as those in time-step $k+1$.
elements.sent-2411-176: Hence this model has overlapping generations.
elements.sent-2411-177: When $\alpha_N$ is equal to a fixed $p$ in $[0,1)$, our model is exactly that in \citet{linder2009 .}
elements.subsection-2411-005: The pedigree as important partial information of the population
elements.sent-2411-178: The above population dynamics generates a random graph, which we will call $\mathcal{G _N$, with vertex set $I \times \mathbb{Z _{+}$ (individuals) and directed edges joining each individual with its parent(s).}}
elements.sent-2411-179: This random graph $\mathcal{G _N$ is called the pedigree of the population.}
elements.sent-2411-180: In the following, we consider the pedigree without specifying outcomes of genetic transmission.
elements.sent-2411-181: Between consecutive time-steps with two distinct parents, the pedigree has one edge from offspring $(\kappa_k, k)$ to parent $(\pi_{k,1 , k + 1)$, one from offspring $(\kappa_k, k)$ to parent $(\pi_{k,2 , k + 1)$, and $N - 1$ single edges for the same individual from $(j, k)$ to $(j, k + 1)$ for $j \in I \setminus \{\kappa_k\}$.}}
elements.sent-2411-182: The portion of the pedigree corresponding to the example above is \begin{equation}\label{Fig: Moran_non_selfing} \xymatrix@=1.8em@C=1.8em { {( \pi_{k,1} = 2, \pi_{k,2} = 4) \quad \text{time-step }k+1} 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 {({ \kappa_k = 3}) \qquad \text{time-step }k} 1 \ar[u]\ar[u] 2 \ar[u]\ar[u] 3 \ar[ul]\ar[ur] 4 \ar[u]\ar[u] 5\ar[u]\ar[u] 6\ar[u]\ar[u] 7\ar[u]\ar[u] }\end{equation} Between consecutive time-steps with selfing, the pedigree has a double edges from $(\kappa_k, k)$ to $(\pi_{k , k + 1)$, and again $N - 1$ single edges from $(j, k)$ to $(j, k + 1)$ for $j \in I \setminus \{\kappa_k\}$.}
elements.sent-2411-183: The portion of the pedigree corresponding to the example above is \begin{equation}\label{Fig: Moran_selfing} \xymatrix@=1.8em@C=1.8em { {\qquad \qquad\; (\pi_{k} =4 ) \quad \text{time-step }k+1} 1 2 3 4 5 6 7 {(\kappa_k = 3) \qquad \text{time-step } k} 1 \ar[u]\ar[u] 2 \ar[u]\ar[u] 3 \ar@/_/[ur]\ar[ur] 4 \ar[u]\ar[u] 5 \ar[u]\ar[u] 6 \ar[u]\ar[u] 7\ar[u]\ar[u] }\end{equation} Hence, the offspring always has 2 edges coming out of it which trace upward (towards the past); and all the other $N-1$ individuals have a single edge.
elements.sent-2411-184: Repetition of this process with these two possibilities for each time step backward into the indefinite past results in a single realized pedigree of the population.
elements.sent-2411-185: Patterns of genetic ancestry at each locus are outcomes of Mendelian transmission conditional on this one pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-186: Unlinked loci are transmitted independently through the pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-187: In Figure~\ref{fig:sample_genealogy , we illustrate a realization of both the population dynamics with genetic transmission and the corresponding pedigree for the first 4 generations in the past for a population of size $6$.}
elements.figure-2411-002: \includegraphics[scale=0.6]{sample_genealogy.png} \caption{ A realization of our diploid Moran process with $N = 6$ individuals (left image) and the corresponding pedigree (right image). On the left, two genes $(X_0, Y_0) = (2, 9)$ are sampled in the present time-step $0$, and their lineages are highlighted by the solid dots. Namely, $\{(X_k, Y_k)\}_{k=0}^4=\{(2,9), (2,9), (2,5), (6,5), (4,4)\}$. These two lineages coalesce in time-step $4$ because $X_4=Y_4$ but $X_k\neq Y_k$ for $k=0,1,2,3$.} \label{fig:sample_genealogy}
elements.subsection-2411-006: Pairwise coalescence time: unconditional versus conditional on the pedigree
elements.sent-2411-188: In our population model each of the $N$ individuals has two copies of each genetic locus.
elements.sent-2411-189: Suppose we number these $J = \{1,...,2N\}$ such that the individual with label $i\in I$ has gene copies $ 2i-1$ and $2i$.
elements.sent-2411-190: Broadly, our aim is to describe the statistical properties of random samples of gene copies from $J$ by following their ancestral genetic lineages backward in time.
elements.sent-2411-191: Suppose we sample two gene copies $X_0$ and $Y_0$ without replacement from the $2N$ present at time $k=0$.
elements.sent-2411-192: Let $X_k$ and $Y_k$ be their ancestral lineages $k$ time-steps in the past.
elements.sent-2411-193: Then $(X_k)_{k\in\Z_+}$ and $(Y_k)_{k\in\Z_+}$ are two correlated Markov chains with state space $J$ that have the same transition probabilities.
elements.sent-2411-194: Our main object of study, for sample size $n=2$, is \begin{equation}\label{Def:tauN2} \tau^{(N,2)}:= \inf\{k\in\Z_+:\,X_k = Y_k\},\end{equation} the (pairwise) coalescence time of the sample of two genes.
elements.sent-2411-195: The left image in Figure~\ref{fig:sample_genealogy shows a realization of the population process in which $\tau^{(6,2) =4$.}}
elements.sent-2411-196: The value of $\tau^{(N,2)}$ is completely determined by the population process.
elements.sent-2411-197: However, knowing the pedigree is {\it not enough to determine the exact value of $\tau^{(N,2)}$, due to Mendelian randomness.}
elements.sent-2411-198: For instance, even if we know the pedigree is exactly the one the right in Figure~\ref{fig:sample_genealogy and we know that $X_0=2$, we do not know what $X_3$ is: $X_3$ can be in one of the genes $\{5,6,7,8\}$ with equal probability.}
elements.section-2411-003: Unconditional distribution of pairwise coalescence time
elements.sent-2411-199: The unconditional distribution of $\tau^{(N,2)}$ (i.e. averaging over the randomness of the pedigree) can be obtained by considering a Markov process with 3 states $\{\text{diff ,\, \text{same , \,\text{coal}\}$ representing respectively that the two gene copies are in two difference individuals, the gene copies are in the same individual but have not coalesced, and the gene copies have coalesced. \begin{equation}{ \setlength{\arraycolsep}{3pt} diff =  {\color{blue} \bullet }   {\color{teal} \bullet }  \, same =  {\color{blue} \bullet } {\color{teal} \bullet } ,\ coal= {\color{cyan} \bullet }  }\end{equation} The one-step transition matrix $\mathbf{\Pi _N$ of this Markov process, under our diploid Moran model, is \begin{center $\mathbf{\Pi _N\,:=\,$ \begin{tabular {c|ccc $\text{diff}$ $\text{same}$ $\text{coal}$ $\text{diff}$ $1 - \frac{2 {N(N-1)}$ $\frac{1 {N(N-1)}$ $\frac{1 {N(N-1)}$ $\text{same}$ $(1-\alpha_N) \frac{1 {N}$ $\frac{N-1 {N + \alpha_N \frac{1 {2N}$ $\alpha_N \frac{1 {2N}$ $\text{coal}$ $0$ $0$ $1$ \end{tabular \end{center For $q\in \{ \text{diff ,\,\text{same , \,\text{coal}\}$, we let $\mathbb P_q$ be the probability under which the initial state (i.e. the sampling configurations) is $q$.}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}}

elements.figure-2411-004: \includegraphics[scale=0.37]{combined_figure.png} \caption{ The limiting conditional CDF $t\mapsto \lim_{N\to\infty}\Pdiff(N^{-2}\tau^{(N,2)} \leq t | \mathcal{A}_N)$ for our three assumptions about $\alpha_N$: partial selfing, limited outcrossing, and negligible outcrossing. elements.figure-2411-007: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{pedigree_limit.png} \caption{ (Left) A realization $G_\lambda$ of the random ancestral graph $G_{\lambda}$ starting with two particles/nodes. For this particular realization, the overlap times of the sample lineages are $t_1< t_2< t_3$. (Right) The ``random staircase'' mentioned in Table~\ref{tab:summary_of_results} corresponding to this realization of $G_\lambda$. The conditional distribution of the limiting coalescence time $T_\lambda$, given $G_\lambda$, is obtained by tracing ancestral genetic lineages backwards in time along the graph $G_\lambda$. Given this $G_\lambda$, $T_\lambda$ must take values in $\{t_1, t_2, t_3\}$. One can read off the conditional CDF of $T_\lambda$ as follows: The node of $G_{\lambda}$ at time $t_1$ contains $\frac{3}{4}$ of the genetics of the right lineage and the whole of that of the left lineage, so $\mathbb{P}(T_\lambda=t_1 | G_\lambda ) = \frac{3}{4}$. Between $t_1$ and $t_2$, half of the left lineage splits into the left handle. At $t_2$, $\frac{1}{4}$ of the right lineage meets half of the left lineage, so $\mathbb{P}(T = t_2 | G_\lambda) = \frac{1}{8}$. The remainder of the right lineage meets the remainder of the left lineage at $t_3$ so $\mathbb{P}(T_\lambda = t_3) = \frac{1}{8}$.} \label{fig: pedigree_limit}

elements.sent-2411-661: In this work, we have assumed that the pedigree of a population of constant size $N$ is the outcome of a random process of reproduction in which offspring are produced by self-fertilization with probability $\alpha_N$.
elements.sent-2411-662: By conditioning on the pedigree and considering pairwise times to common ancestry, we found three different coalescent models in the limit $N\to\infty$, when time is measured in units of $N$ generations (which is $N^2$ time-steps in the Moran model).
elements.sent-2411-663: The deciding factor is $N(1-\alpha_N)$, which may be interpreted either as the rate of outcrossing events along an ancestral lineage or as the expected number of outcrossed offspring in the population each generation.
elements.sent-2411-664: The three models are `negligible outcrossing' which applies when $N(1-\alpha_N)\to0$, `limited outcrossing' which applies when $N(1-\alpha_N)\to\lambda\in(0,\infty)$, and `partial selfing' which applies when $N(1-\alpha_N)\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-665: Negligible outcrossing and limited outcrossing both require $\alpha_N \to 1$, while partial selfing includes this as a special case.
elements.sent-2411-666: Previous population-genetic analyses of selfing populations have assumed a fixed selfing rate, $\alpha_N=\alpha$ in our notation, and have implicitly averaged over pedigrees.
elements.sent-2411-667: When this averaging is done, just one coalescent model for all $\alpha\in[0,1]$ emerges in the limit, specifically the coalescent model with partial selfing described by \citet{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 and \citet{Mohle1998a .}}
elements.sent-2411-668: When coalescence is conditioned on the pedigree and $\alpha$ is fixed, our very similar partial-selfing model is obtained but only for $\alpha\in[0,1)$.
elements.sent-2411-669: Assuming a fixed selfing rate of $\alpha=1$ leads to an entirely different model, namely negligible outcrossing.
elements.sent-2411-670: A more detailed look at how $\alpha_N$ approaches $1$ reveals the critical case of limited outcrossing in between negligible outcrossing and partial selfing and characterized by the parameter $N(1-\alpha_N)\to\lambda\in(0,\infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-671: In this regime, the parts of the population pedigree directly ancestral to the sampled individuals can be replaced by a random graph like the ones previously used to model recombination \citep{Griffiths1991,GriffithsAndMarjoram1997 and selection \citep{kroneneuhauser1997,NeuhauserAndKrone1997 .}}
elements.sent-2411-672: It is distinguished from these only by what the splitting events in the graph represent and how these are treated in modeling gene genealogies.
elements.sent-2411-673: Each splitting event in the graph corresponds to an outcrossing event in the genealogical ancestry of the sampled individuals.
elements.sent-2411-674: Ancestral lineages tracing back to such a node in the graph make a $50$:$50$ choice of which branch they follow.
elements.sent-2411-675: The rate at which ancestral lineages split is precisely our parameter $\lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-676: As with the well known Wright-Fisher diffusion and the corresponding standard neutral coalescent model, these new models of coalescence conditional on the pedigree are meant as robust approximations for large populations.

elements.sent-2411-819: We study how the global graph of reproductive relationships, or population pedigree, influences the genealogical relationships of a sample of two gene copies at a genetic locus.
elements.sent-2411-820: The distribution of coalescence times obtained in this way predicts variation among unlinked loci in a sample of individuals.
elements.sent-2411-821: Traditional coalescent analyses implicitly average over pedigrees and generally make different predictions.
elements.sent-2411-822: In the case of limited outcrossing, the retained pedigree information forms a random graph, with coalescence times given by the meeting times of random walks on this graph.
elements.sent-2411-823: The organismal genealogy of all individuals in a population for all time, also known as the population pedigree, has not traditionally been of concern in population genetics.
elements.sent-2411-824: But simulation studies, beginning with \citet{ball1990 and including \citet{kuoavise2008 , \citet{WakeleyEtAl2016 and \citet{WiltonEtAl2017 , have suggested that the population pedigree can be important in structuring genetic variation.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-825: Of course, some of the earliest work in population genetics concerned how pedigrees affect patterns of variation, specifically identity by descent \citep{Wright1921a,Wright1921b,Wright1921c,Wright1921d,Wright1921e,Wright1922 .}
elements.sent-2411-826: But the pedigrees in these early works were either ones laid down by regular systems of mating---see \citet{Lachance2009 for a recent treatment---or pedigrees of arbitrary structure extending back a limited number of generations.}
elements.sent-2411-827: These latter, relatively small pedigrees were assumed to be embedded in an infinite population with global properties, such as Hardy-Weinberg equilibrium and no identity by descent, and within which there was no explicit accounting of individuals or their relationships.
elements.sent-2411-828: Finite population models including coalescent models are models of identity by descent.
elements.sent-2411-829: But population pedigrees did not figure in their development.
elements.sent-2411-830: The approach instead was to average over pedigrees by averaging over the outcomes of reproduction in each generation.
elements.sent-2411-831: See \citet{DFBW24 for more about this history and a discussion of the roles of pedigrees in forward-time versus backward-time models.}
elements.sent-2411-832: Averaging over pedigrees is usually done implicitly, as in the statement that two ancestral lines coalesce with probability $1/(2N)$ each generation under the diploid monoecious Wright-Fisher model.
elements.sent-2411-833: It is done explicitly, for example, in equation (4.1) in \citet{kingman1982 for the coalescence probability under Cannings' haploid exchangeable model \citep{Cannings1974 .}}
elements.sent-2411-834: Predictions for diploid organisms based on this traditional approach are marginal to the population pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-835: Coalescent theory describes the ancestry of gene copies or alleles in a sample of individuals from a population.
elements.sent-2411-836: Coalescent models are often applied by comparing predicted distributions of genetic variation at a single locus to data from multiple unlinked loci in a sample of individuals.
elements.sent-2411-837: This is done for example in analyses of the site-frequency spectrum \citep{Tajima1989,BravermanEtAl1995,Fu1995,Achaz2009,GutenkunstEtAl2009,GaoAndKeinan2016 or when likelihoods are multiplied across unlinked loci \citep{Watterson1985,Wakeley1999,Nielsen2000,AdamsAndHudson2004,WangAndHey2010,LohseEtAl2011,CWH2017,CWH2021 .}}
elements.sent-2411-838: But a sample of individuals has a single shared pedigree, fixed in time by past outcomes of reproduction.
elements.sent-2411-839: Further, in large populations with substantial outcrossing, the pedigree of any sample of individuals quickly spreads out to encompass the whole population \citep{Chang1999,DerridaEtAl1999,Lachance2009,BartonAndEtheridge2011 .}
elements.sent-2411-840: So it is important to know whether any specific features of the population pedigree might affect our predictions about genetic variation.
elements.sent-2411-841: Coalescent models conditional on the pedigree give the correct sampling structure for multiple loci in a sample of individuals.
elements.sent-2411-842: In particular, unlinked loci represent conditionally independent realizations of the process of genetic transmission within the same fixed pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-843: Methods such as multiplying likelihoods across unlinked loci are obviously justified when conditioning on the pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-844: Modeling unlinked loci as independent draws from the distributions of traditionally formulated coalescent models implicitly assumes that each locus has its own population pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-845: So far there have been only two analytical treatments of coalescence conditional on the population pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-846: But it is not true in general that extensions of the standard neutral coalescent model are similarly applicable to coalescent processes conditional on pedigrees generated under the corresponding population models.
elements.sent-2411-847: In this work, we consider how coalescence conditional on the population pedigree is affected by autogamy or self-fertilization (hereafter just `selfing').
elements.sent-2411-848: Modes of reproduction are of course fundamental aspects of evolution \citep{CharlesworthAndWright2001,GleminEtAl2019 .}
elements.sent-2411-849: Selfing is when two gametes produced by meiosis by the same individual unite to form a zygote.
elements.sent-2411-850: This type of inbreeding has been especially important in the evolution of plants \citep{Jain1976,UyenoyamaEtAl1993,Charlesworth2006,OlsenEtAl2021 .}
elements.sent-2411-851: The common alternative to selfing in both plants and animals is outcrossing, in which zygotes are formed by the union of two gametes from different individuals.
elements.sent-2411-852: We consider just these two possibilities, and characterize populations by the probability individuals are produced by selfing.
elements.sent-2411-853: We describe three distinct kinds of conditional coalescent models which hold for different magnitudes of the selfing probability.
elements.sent-2411-854: These models exist in the limit as the population size $N$ tends to infinity in the same way that the Wright-Fisher diffusion and the corresponding standard neutral coalescent model exist in this limit \citep{Ewens2004 .}
elements.sent-2411-855: We use $\alpha_N$ to denote the selfing probability, with explicit dependence on $N$ so we can control the importance of selfing in the model.
elements.sent-2411-856: Our results for coalescence in large populations conditional on the pedigree depend strongly on the relative magnitude of $\alpha_N$, specifically how $\alpha_N$ changes as $N\to\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-857: Most previous analyses, in addition to not conditioning on the pedigree, have assumed a fixed selfing probability.
elements.sent-2411-858: This probability is often denoted $s$, though various notations have been adopted; e.g.\ $l$ in \citet{Haldane1924 , $h$ in \citet{Wright1951 , $\beta$ in \citet{Pollak1987 and $\alpha$ in \citet{Karlin1968a,Karlin1968b .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-859: As \citet{Uyenoyama2024 recently emphasized, some authors use $s$ for the entire selfing probability while others use it to measure selfing in excess of a background probability of $1/N$ due to random mating.}
elements.sent-2411-860: Our model includes these as special cases, namely if $\alpha_N = s$ or $\alpha_N = s + (1-s)/N$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-861: Both of these fixed-$s$ models make the same predictions in the limit and both have been called `partial self-fertilization' or `partial selfing'.
elements.sent-2411-862: If outcrossing is more frequent than in the critical case, for example $1-\alpha_N$ of order $1/\sqrt{N}$, the limiting conditional coalescent model resembles the pedigree-averaged or unconditional coalescent model of \citet{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 and \citet{Mohle1998a .}}
elements.sent-2411-863: The latter model holds for all $\alpha\in[0,1]$ as long as the rate of approach to $\alpha=1$ is not too fast, whereas limited outcrossing and negligible outcrossing hold just for $\alpha_N\to 1$.
elements.sent-2411-864: In the next two sections, we review previous works which generally use $s$ for the fixed selfing probability, take $s\in[0,1]$, and do not condition on the population pedigree.
elements.sent-2411-865: In Section~\ref{sec:pedigrees , we explain how previous population-genetic models of selfing in fact average over pedigrees.}
elements.sent-2411-866: In Sections~\ref{sec:broad and~\ref{sec:specific we use $s$ for continuity with what one finds in much of the evolutionary and population-genetic literature.}}
elements.subsection-2411-018: Prevalence and evolutionary-genetic consequences of selfing
elements.sent-2411-867: About $10$-$15$\% of plant species are predominantly selfing \citep{WrightEtAl2013 , as are two of the best-studied genetic model species, Arabidopsis thaliana and Caenorhabditis elegans \citep{AbbottAndGomes1989,SellingerEtAl2020,BarriereAndFelix2005 .}}
elements.sent-2411-868: The empirical distribution of selfing probabilities among plant species is markedly bimodal, with relatively fewer species having $s\in(0.2,0.8)$ \citep{SchemskeAndLande1985,VoglerAndKalisz2001,GoodwillieEtAl2005 .}
elements.sent-2411-869: Whether selfing is favored over outcrossing or vice versa in a given species depends on a number of ecological and evolutionary factors \citep{Fisher1941,Moran1962,LandeAndSchemske1985,Charlesworth2006,Kamran‐DisfaniAndAgrawal2014 .}
elements.sent-2411-870: First, selfing increases single-locus homozygosity via increased identity by descent \citep{Haldane1924,Rousset2002 .}
elements.sent-2411-871: A simple coalescent interpretation is that $F$ is the probability that the two alleles or gene copies at a locus in an individual coalesce in the recent ancestry of that individual, rather than being separated into different individuals by an outcrossing event \citep{NordborgAndDonnelly1997 .}
elements.sent-2411-872: Second, selfing reduces the effective rate of recombination \citep{BennettAndBinet1956,WeirAndCockerham1973,GoldingAndStrobeck1980,VitalisAndCouvet2001 .}
elements.sent-2411-873: Then the same coefficient $F$ is the probability that the recombination event gets healed or repaired by a coalescent event within that parental individual's recent ancestry.
elements.sent-2411-874: So only a fraction $1-F=2(1-s)/(2-s)$ of recombination events is observable.
elements.sent-2411-875: Third, selfing generates non-random associations of diploid genotypes at different loci, even if the loci are unlinked \citep{Haldane1949,BennettAndBinet1956,Kimura1958,Narain1966,CockerhamAndWeir1968,CockerhamAndWeir1977,CockerhamAndWeir1983,CockerhamAndWeir1984,Smith2023,Uyenoyama2024 .}

elements.figure-2411-014: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{CDF_ancestral_graph.jpg} \caption{ (Left) A realization of the random ancestral graph $G_{\lambda}$ starting with two particles/nodes. For this particular realization, the overlap times of the sample lineages are $t_1< t_2< t_3$. (Right) The CDF corresponding to this $G_\lambda$. The conditional distribution of the limiting coalescence time $T_\lambda$, given $G_\lambda$, is obtained by tracing ancestral genetic lineages backwards in time along the graph $G_\lambda$. Given this $G_\lambda$, $T_\lambda$ must take values in $\{t_1, t_2, t_3\}$. One can read off the conditional CDF of $T_\lambda$ as follows, by tracing the ancestries of a hypothetically infinite number of unlinked loci. The node of $G_{\lambda}$ at time $t_1$ contains $3/4$ of the ancestries from the right sample lineage and the whole of those from the left sample lineage, so $\mathbb{P}(T_\lambda=t_1 | G_\lambda ) = 3/4$. Between $t_1$ and $t_2$, half of the loci following the left ancestral lineage go left at the split and the other $1/2$ continue up. The lineage on the right between $t_1$ and $t_2$ contains $1/4$ of the ancestries from the right sample lineage. So when these two lineages overlap at $t_2$ we have $\mathbb{P}(T_\lambda = t_2 | G_\lambda) = 1/8$. Finally the remainder of the right sample lineage meets the remainder of the left sample lineage at $t_3$ for $\mathbb{P}(T_\lambda = t_3) = 1/8$.} \label{fig: pedigree_limit}

elements.sent-2411-1001: To understand how information relevant to conditional gene genealogies can persist even in the limit $N \to \infty$, we introduce a discrete-time ancestral graph, $G^N = \left(G^N(k)\right)_{k \in \Z_+}$, which is a subset of the pedigree containing all potential individuals through which our sample of $n=2$ gene copies may have been transmitted.
elements.sent-2411-1002: Each realization of $G^N$ is constructed by the following two rules.
elements.sent-2411-1003: We refer to potential ancestral individuals of the sample as nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1004: When a node is the result of an outcrossing event, it fragments into two nodes.
elements.sent-2411-1005: When one node is the offspring of another node in a selfing event, the two coagulate into a single node.
elements.sent-2411-1006: Individual genetic lineages are transmitted through this graph according to Mendel's laws as in Section~\ref{section: moran_model .}
elements.sent-2411-1007: A realization of $G^N$ is shown in Figure~\ref{fig: discrete_ancestral_graph .. \FloatBarrier.}
elements.figure-2411-015: \includegraphics[scale=0.4]{discrete_ancestral_graph_bis.jpg} \caption{ A realization of $G^N$ containing potential ancestors of the two sampled individuals (ovals) at the bottom. In past time-step $3$, the ancestral individual on the left undergoes an outcrossing event. In past time-step $4$, one of the nodes from this event is the offspring of one of the potential ancestral individuals of the sampled individual on the right, and these two nodes coagulate. In the fifth time-step, the right most particle experiences an outcrossing in which exactly one of the parents is a node in the graph. This may appear in the discrete-time ancestral graph but will not occur in the limiting ancestral graph.} \label{fig: discrete_ancestral_graph}
elements.sent-2411-1008: For the random variable $G^N$ to have a suitable limit as the population size goes to infinity we require that the rate at which nodes fragment, approximately $(1-\alpha_N)N^{-1}$, and the rate at which pairs of nodes coagulate, of order $N^{-2}$, are comparable.
elements.sent-2411-1009: This comparability is taken to mean that the ratio of these two rates, $N(1-\alpha_N)$, converges as $N$ goes to infinity to $\lambda \in \R_+$.
